text: The surgeon uses a permanent suture to close the anterior defect, ensuring the suture catches bits of mesh left in place.
The surgeon begins suturing at the bottom of the hernia defect, ensuring a reasonable overlap of the mesh behind or below the defect.
The surgeon has nearly used up the entire suture while closing the anterior defect of the hernia.
The surgeon attempts to imbricate the hernia sac, folding and suturing it to reduce dead space within the surgical field.
The surgeon parks the needle temporarily and begins suturing from the top of the incision.
The surgeon begins the procedure just underneath the xiphoid process, indicating its location at the top of the surgical field.
The surgeon decreases the pressure to eight while closing the defect, ensuring the tension is managed as the tissue comes together.
The surgeon's assistant applies downward pressure on the hernia sac to facilitate the surgeon in taking precise bites during the closure of the defect.
The surgeon sequentially tightens the sutures with each throw to close the hernia defect, ensuring even tension distribution across the closure.
The surgeon places a new stitch in the center of the hernia repair site, aiming to bring the tissue together and offload tension as the closure progresses from the top and bottom.
The surgeon cinches the sutures, visibly bringing the tissue edges together to close the hernia defect.
The surgeon continues to tighten the sutures from the top, progressively bringing the tissue edges closer together.
The surgeon continues to close the hernia defect, visibly restoring the rectus muscles towards the midline as the tissue edges are brought together.
The surgeon continues to use each suture until its end, progressively tightening the closure of the hernia defect.
The surgeon closes the defect by gradually releasing tension, bringing the edges of the tissue together.

The surgeon brings the rectus muscles together, closing the defect. The hernia sac is imbricated, with sutures ensuring it touches the top of the rectus muscles anteriorly, revealing redundancy in the suturing.
The surgeon sutures the linea alba together, incorporating pieces of mesh into the closure.
The surgeon places an additional suture at the bottom of the hernia repair site, completing the closure of the defect.
The surgeon identifies a small gap in the sutured area and begins placing an additional suture in the middle to close the gap.
The surgeon displays the multiple suture holes made on the left superior side of the posterior sheath, highlighting the area where a gap was previously present.
The surgeon demonstrates the completed closure of the hernia defect, showing the neatly aligned tissue after running a 3-0 B-lock suture to secure all defects together.
The surgeon uses extra suture material to close a defect located above the liver, ensuring the area is securely closed despite initial uncertainty about the necessity of closure.
The surgeon identifies another defect adjacent to the previously closed area and proceeds to close it using sutures.

The surgeon demonstrates the closure of trocar site defects in the left upper quadrant, ensuring all holes along the left-hand side are closed. The surgical instruments are redocked on the opposite side to continue closure.
The surgeon closes a defect on the patient's right side, using sutures to secure the tissue and prevent potential hernia formation.

The surgeon closes the posterior elements starting at the top, with the camera docked on the patient's left side. The view is oriented towards the xiphoid process, showing the diaphragm in motion. The posterior opening along the midline is being sutured, with the tissue coming together smoothly and without tension.
The posterior sheath sits flat along the viscera without tension, indicating successful closure and preventing potential complications.
The surgeon measures the hernia defect, assessing its size and dimensions within the surgical field.
The surgeon measures the hernia defect, noting its dimensions as approximately forty centimeters in the longitudinal axis and thirty-three to thirty-four centimeters in the horizontal axis.
The surgeon upsizes the left upper quadrant port to a twelve-millimeter size to facilitate the insertion of the mesh, which has been cut to approximately thirty-eight by thirty-three centimeters.
The surgeon unfolds the mesh laparoscopically, maneuvering the camera to view both sides of the abdomen as the mesh is positioned.

The surgeon laparoscopically unfolds the mesh, adjusting the camera through a different port to view the right upper quadrant, ensuring the mesh is unrolled and lays completely flat against the abdominal wall.

The surgeon closes the left upper quadrant 12mm port site using a suture passer. A drain is positioned exiting from the left lower quadrant port, centered in place. The surgical field is then desufflated.
